items.title: Laravel Lang Compromised with RCE Backdoor Across 700+ Versions
items.description: Community-maintained Laravel Lang packages were compromised with remote code execution backdoors affecting over 700 versions across multiple repositories including laravel-lang/lang, laravel-lang/http-statuses, laravel-lang/attributes, and laravel-lang/actions. The attack involved coordinated rapid tag publishing on May 22-23, 2026, suggesting organization-level credential compromise. A malicious
items.ai_summary: The Laravel Lang package compromise involves a remote code execution (RCE) backdoor affecting over 700 versions across multiple repositories. The attack vector involves exploiting public-facing applications (T1190) by leveraging compromised credentials, allowing threat actors to execute malicious commands and scripts (T1059) on affected systems. Real-world impact includes potential data breaches and unauthorized access to sensitive information, with organizations using the compromised packages being most at risk. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove and replace the compromised packages with clean versions, ensuring all dependencies are updated to the latest secure versions. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity and implement security controls to prevent similar exploits, such as restricting access to public-facing applications and regularly reviewing credential access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Android Trojan Abuses Commercial Rooting Tool and Steals Private Information
items.description: Rootnik is an Android trojan that exploits vulnerabilities in Android 4.3 and earlier by weaponizing a Chinese commercial rooting tool called Root Assistant. The malicious operation spreads through repackaged legitimate applications distributed globally, affecting users primarily in the United States, Malaysia, Thailand, Lebanon and Taiwan. After installation, Rootnik gains root access using stole
items.ai_summary: The Rootnik Android trojan exploits vulnerabilities in Android 4.3 and earlier versions by utilizing a commercial rooting tool called Root Assistant, allowing it to gain root access and steal private information. The trojan spreads through repackaged legitimate applications, primarily affecting users in the United States, Malaysia, Thailand, Lebanon, and Taiwan. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure their Android devices are updated to at least version 4.4, and avoid installing applications from untrusted sources. Additionally, users should be cautious when granting root access to applications and monitor their device for suspicious activity. Defenders can also implement application whitelisting and regularly scan for malware to prevent similar threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1068| B[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    B -->|T1212| C[Exploitation for Credential Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1414| D[Input Capture]:::tactic
    D -->|T1532| E[Data Storage]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Exploitation of KnowledgeDeliver via ViewState Deserialization Vulnerability
items.description: In late 2025, an unknown threat actor exploited a critical zero-day vulnerability in KnowledgeDeliver, a Learning Management System widely used in Japan. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-5426, allowed unauthenticated remote code execution through ViewState deserialization attacks. The issue stemmed from identical hardcoded ASP.NET machine keys distributed across multiple customer deployments
items.ai_summary: The exploitation of KnowledgeDeliver via ViewState deserialization vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-5426, is a critical zero-day vulnerability that allows unauthenticated remote code execution. This vulnerability affects multiple customer deployments of KnowledgeDeliver, a Learning Management System widely used in Japan, due to identical hardcoded ASP.NET machine keys. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to a full system compromise, allowing threat actors to execute arbitrary code, steal sensitive data, and disrupt the learning management system. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should immediately update to the latest version of KnowledgeDeliver, change the ASP.NET machine keys to unique values for each deployment, and restrict access to the system to only trusted IP addresses. Additionally, defenders should monitor system logs for suspicious activity and implement a web application firewall to detect and prevent similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059| C[Command and Scripting Interpreter]:::tactic
    C -->|T1210| D[Exploitation for Client Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Token Bingo: Don't Let Your Code be the Winner
items.description: In early April 2026, a large-scale device code phishing campaign targeted organizations across multiple sectors and regions, exploiting OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant. Threat actors leveraged the Kali365 phishing-as-a-service platform, originating primarily from IP address 216.203.20[.]95. The campaign used high-fidelity lures directing victims to Microsoft's legitimate device login flow, wh
items.ai_summary: The Token Bingo phishing campaign, detected in early April 2026, exploits the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant, targeting organizations across multiple sectors. This campaign leverages the Kali365 phishing-as-a-service platform, primarily originating from IP address 216.203.20[.]95, and uses high-fidelity lures to direct victims to Microsoft's legitimate device login flow. The attack vector involves phishing (T1566) and user execution (T1204), where victims unknowingly grant access to their device codes, potentially leading to unauthorized access to sensitive information. To remediate, defenders should implement strict verification processes for device login flows, monitor for suspicious activity from the identified IP address, and educate users about the risks of phishing campaigns. Additionally, defenders can configure their systems to block traffic from the malicious IP address and implement multi-factor authentication to prevent unauthorized access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: RemotePE: The Lazarus RAT that lives in memory
items.description: A sophisticated memory-only toolset used by a North Korean Lazarus subgroup targeting financial and cryptocurrency organizations consists of three malware families forming a chain. DPAPILoader decrypts and loads RemotePELoader from disk using Windows Data Protection API. RemotePELoader beacons to command-and-control servers and retrieves RemotePE, a fully-fledged remote access trojan executed enti
items.ai_summary: The RemotePE threat involves a sophisticated memory-only toolset used by the North Korean Lazarus subgroup, targeting financial and cryptocurrency organizations. This toolset consists of three malware families: DPAPILoader, RemotePELoader, and RemotePE, which form a chain to execute a fully-fledged remote access trojan in memory. The attack vector involves the use of the Windows Data Protection API to decrypt and load RemotePELoader from disk, which then beacons to command-and-control servers to retrieve RemotePE. If exploited, this threat could result in significant financial losses for affected organizations, as it allows for unauthorized access to sensitive systems and data. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust network monitoring and detection capabilities to identify suspicious beaconing activity, and ensure that all systems are configured to prevent the use of the Windows Data Protection API for malicious purposes, such as by restricting access to sensitive APIs and implementing strict input validation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1071| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Phishing Campaign Deploys JavaScript-Driven PureLogs Variant to Steal Sensitive Data
items.description: A sophisticated phishing campaign distributes a PureLogs variant through deceptive purchase order emails containing malicious JavaScript files. The attack chain employs obfuscated JavaScript that drops PowerShell scripts, which then use process hollowing techniques to inject .NET modules into legitimate Windows processes. The malware communicates with command-and-control infrastructure to download
items.ai_summary: The phishing campaign utilizes JavaScript-driven PureLogs variant to steal sensitive data, specifically targeting Windows systems through deceptive purchase order emails containing malicious JavaScript files. The attack chain involves obfuscated JavaScript that drops PowerShell scripts, employing process hollowing techniques to inject .NET modules into legitimate Windows processes, such as explorer.exe or dllhost.exe. If exploited, this campaign can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data, including login credentials and financial information, affecting various organizations and individuals. To remediate, defenders should implement email filters to block suspicious attachments, restrict PowerShell execution to authorized personnel, and monitor system logs for unusual process activity, updating their security software to the latest versions, such as PowerShell 7.2 or later. Additionally, defenders can configure Windows Defender to detect and block the PureLogs variant by enabling the 'Block obfuscated scripts' feature and setting the 'Script scanning' option to 'Enabled'.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.007| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1055| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Smart Contracts for C&C: How ClearFake Hid in Plain Sight on BSC Testnet
items.description: Threat actors exploited the EtherHiding technique to store ClearFake payload routing instructions within smart contracts on the BNB Smart Chain testnet, creating an immutable command-and-control infrastructure that cannot be taken down. The attack began with injected JavaScript on a compromised Swiss website that queried blockchain contracts to deliver malicious payloads. Victims passing anti-anal
items.ai_summary: The ClearFake campaign exploited the EtherHiding technique to store payload routing instructions within smart contracts on the BNB Smart Chain testnet, creating an immutable command-and-control infrastructure. This attack began with injected JavaScript on a compromised Swiss website, which queried blockchain contracts to deliver malicious payloads. The use of smart contracts for C&C infrastructure allows the threat actors to maintain persistence and evade detection, as these contracts cannot be taken down. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust input validation and sanitization for any web applications that interact with blockchain contracts, and monitor for suspicious JavaScript injection on their websites. Additionally, defenders should utilize tools that can detect and analyze smart contract interactions to identify potential malicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1071| B[Command and Control]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1499| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1566| E[Initial Access]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: From poisoned search results to GPU mining: A cryptojacking campaign abusing ScreenConnect and Microsoft .NET utilities
items.description: Microsoft Defender Experts identified an active cryptojacking campaign leveraging AI-assisted delivery mechanisms alongside traditional SEO poisoning. Attackers create fake download sites impersonating trusted utilities like CrystalDiskInfo, HWMonitor, and FurMark, targeting users with high-performance GPUs. Victims download ZIP archives containing legitimate executables bundled with malicious DLL
items.ai_summary: The cryptojacking campaign exploits users by creating fake download sites that impersonate trusted utilities such as CrystalDiskInfo, HWMonitor, and FurMark, targeting those with high-performance GPUs. The attackers leverage SEO poisoning (T1608.006) to increase the visibility of these fake sites in search results. Once a victim downloads the ZIP archive, they unknowingly install malicious DLLs alongside legitimate executables, which then facilitate the cryptojacking process. To remediate, defenders should implement strict controls on software downloads, ensuring that only trusted sources are used and that all executables are scanned for malicious content before execution. Additionally, monitoring system resource usage for signs of unauthorized GPU utilization can help in early detection of such campaigns.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1608.006| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1560.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1496| D[Resource Hijacking]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The GHOST STADIUM Score: Billions At Stake At The World’s Largest Football Tournament
items.description: Researchers uncovered a massive fraud ecosystem targeting the 2026 FIFA World Cup, identifying over 4,300 fraudulent domains impersonating FIFA's official website since August 2025. At the center operates GHOST STADIUM, a Chinese-speaking threat actor running a sophisticated phishing campaign across 300+ domains using a pixel-perfect clone of FIFA's authentication system. The operation harvests cr
items.ai_summary: The GHOST STADIUM threat actor has been identified as the mastermind behind a massive phishing campaign targeting the 2026 FIFA World Cup, with over 4,300 fraudulent domains impersonating FIFA's official website. The attack vector involves a sophisticated phishing campaign using a pixel-perfect clone of FIFA's authentication system, allowing the threat actor to harvest credentials from unsuspecting victims. If exploited, this could lead to significant financial losses and identity theft for individuals and organizations alike, with billions at stake. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering solutions, conduct regular security awareness training for employees, and ensure that all employees are cautious when interacting with emails or websites related to the FIFA World Cup. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious domain registrations and report any phishing attempts to the relevant authorities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold

items.title: Extortion in the Enterprise: Defending Against BlackFile Attacks
items.description: Since February 2026, multiple incidents involving data theft and extortion have been attributed to activity cluster CL-CRI-1116, also known as BlackFile, UNC6671, and Cordial Spider. These financially-motivated attackers, likely associated with "The Com" collective, employ voice-based phishing combined with credential harvesting through fraudulent login pages. They impersonate IT support staff to
items.ai_summary: The BlackFile threat actor, also known as UNC6671 and Cordial Spider, has been attributed to multiple incidents of data theft and extortion since February 2026. The attackers employ voice-based phishing tactics, often impersonating IT support staff, to trick victims into divulging sensitive credentials through fraudulent login pages. This initial access vector, categorized as T1566 Phishing, allows the attackers to establish a foothold within the target organization. To defend against these attacks, organizations should implement multi-factor authentication, particularly for remote access and sensitive data repositories, and ensure that all employees are aware of the risks associated with voice-based phishing and credential harvesting. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity, such as unusual login attempts or data exfiltration, which may indicate T1041 Exfiltration Over C2 Channel tactics.

graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic

items.description: A vulnerability was identified in continuedev continue up to 1.2.22. This affects the function lsTool of the file core/tools/implementations/lsTool.ts of the component JSON-RPC Server. Such manipulation of the argument dirPath leads to path traversal. An attack has to be approached locally. The exploit is publicly available and might be used. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosure bu
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-8770 is a path traversal vulnerability in the continuedev continue up to version 1.2.22, specifically affecting the lsTool function in the JSON-RPC Server component. This vulnerability can be exploited locally, allowing an attacker to manipulate the dirPath argument and potentially access sensitive files. The exploit is publicly available, which increases the risk of exploitation. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to a version later than 1.2.22 and ensure that the JSON-RPC Server is properly configured to restrict access to sensitive files. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity related to the T1190 and T1486 tactics, which may indicate an active exploitation attempt.

This manipulation causes resource consumption. The attack may be initiated remotely. The exploit has been publicly disclosed and may be utilized. The vendo
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-8769 is a vulnerability in Vercel AI up to version 3.0.97, specifically affecting the createJsonResponseHandler and createJsonErrorResponseHandler functions in the provider-utils component. This issue allows for resource consumption via remote manipulation, potentially leading to denial-of-service conditions. The vulnerability has been publicly disclosed and may be exploited by threat actors, with the CVSS score of 4.3 indicating a moderate severity level. To remediate, users should update to Vercel AI version 3.0.98 or later, ensuring the provider-utils component is updated to mitigate the vulnerability. Additionally, monitoring for unusual resource consumption patterns can help defenders detect potential exploitation attempts.

items.title: CVE-2026-8768: A vulnerability was found in vercel ai up to 3.0.97. The affected element is the...
items.description: A vulnerability was found in vercel ai up to 3.0.97. The affected element is the function validateDownloadUrl of the file packages/provider-utils/src/download-blob.ts of the component provider-utils. The manipulation results in server-side request forgery. The attack can be launched remotely. The exploit has been made public and could be used. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosure b
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-8768 is a server-side request forgery (SSRF) vulnerability in Vercel AI up to version 3.0.97. The vulnerability is located in the function validateDownloadUrl of the file packages/provider-utils/src/download-blob.ts in the provider-utils component. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by manipulating the validateDownloadUrl function to make a malicious request to a public-facing application, resulting in remote code execution. This vulnerability has been made public and can be used by threat actors to launch attacks. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update to version 3.0.98 or later, and ensure that the validateDownloadUrl function is properly validated to prevent SSRF attacks. Additionally, users should implement a web application firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent malicious requests.

items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-8767 is a vulnerability in Vercel AI up to version 3.0.97, specifically affecting the function 'run' in the '.github/workflows/prettier-on-automerge.yml' file. items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-8766 is a flaw in Kilo-Org kilocode up to version 7.0.47, specifically in the Load function of the Environment Variable Handler component. items.title: CVE-2026-8721: Crypt::OpenSSL::PKCS12 versions through 1.94 for Perl truncates passwords with e...
items.description:
Crypt::OpenSSL::PKCS12 versions through 1.94 for Perl truncates passwords with embedded NULLs.

Password parameters in PKCS12.xs are declared char *, which routes through Perl's default typemap to SvPV_nolen.  The Perl length is discarded.

The C code (or OpenSSL internally) calls strlen() on the buffer.  Any password byte at or after the first NULL is silently dropped. Binary / KDF-derived / HMAC

items.title: CVE-2026-8507: Crypt::OpenSSL::PKCS12 versions through 1.94 for Perl have out-of-bounds (OOB) w...
items.description:
Crypt::OpenSSL::PKCS12 versions through 1.94 for Perl have out-of-bounds (OOB) write flaws.

When parsing a PKCS12 file, with a >= 1 GiB OCTET STRING (or BIT STRING) attribute on a SAFEBAG, via info() or info_as_hash(), a heap out-of-bounds write would be triggered with remote-code-execution potential (RCE) due to a signed integer overflow in the size calculation passed to Renew().

items.title:
CVE-2026-46720: Net::Statsd::Tiny versions before 0.3.8 for Perl allowed metric injections.

The...
items.description:
Net::Statsd::Tiny versions before 0.3.8 for Perl allowed metric injections.

The metric names and set values were not checked for newlines, colons or pipes. Metrics generated from untrusted sources could inject additional statsd metrics.

items.title: UK Cyberspying Chief Calls AI ‘an Unstoppable Force’ and Warns About Russia
items.description: The speech is the latest in a string of warnings from intelligence experts that Russia is stepping up hostile activity in a “gray zone” that falls just below the threshold of war. The post UK Cyberspying Chief Calls AI ‘an Unstoppable Force’ and Warns About Russia appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Latin American Cybercriminals Hoover Up Government Data
items.description: A purported leak exposing 5.8 million records of Uruguayan citizens is the latest incident where cybercriminals targeted government agencies to monetize citizen data.

items.title: AI-Assisted Exploit Development Outpaces Scanner Detection
items.description: Attackers are using AI to dramatically reduce the time they need to develop a working exploit for a CVE, according to new research.

items.title: Grandoreiro Malware and BTMOB RAT Campaigns Target Windows and Android Users
items.description: Latin America and Europe become the target of two banking trojan campaigns that are designed to infect Windows and Android devices with Grandoreiro and BTMOB malware, respectively. That's according to new findings from WatchGuard and ESET, which have observed the two malware families being used to single out companies in Spain, Portugal, and Mexico, as well as mobile users in Brazil. The

items.title: Malicious npm Package Stole Files From Claude AI User Directory via GitHub
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered a new malicious package on the npm registry that comes with information stealing capabilities. According to OX Security, the package, named "mouse5212-super-formatter," is designed to upload files from "/mnt/user-data," a dedicated directory used by Anthropic's Claude artificial intelligence (AI) tool to handle uploads and outputs in the background. The

items.title: Rudd orders Cyber Command reviews as Pentagon presses reform agenda
items.description: Army Gen. Joshua Rudd, who took the twin-leadership reins of Cyber Command and the NSA in March, recently tapped MITRE to conduct a potentially wide-ranging review into the organization, according to three people familiar with the matter.

items.title: FBI warns extortion hackers are visiting US law firms to steal data
items.description: In a public advisory issued Tuesday the FBI said a hacking group has targeted law firms using social engineering schemes to gain remote access to corporate systems and exfiltrate data.

items.title: Vulnerability in Popular Conference Software Granted Attackers a 100% Talk Acceptance Rate
items.description: Novee researchers discovered an account takeover vulnerability in the open source CFP management tool Pretalx. The post Vulnerability in Popular Conference Software Granted Attackers a 100% Talk Acceptance Rate appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Can you enforce strong Active Directory password rules without frustrating users?
items.description: Strong Active Directory passwords don't have to come at the expense of usability. Specops Software explains how passphrases, breached password protection, and self-service resets can improve security without frustrating users. [...]

items.title: 5 Steps to Managing Shadow AI Tools Without Slowing Down Employees
items.description: When an employee installs an AI writing assistant, connects a coding copilot to their IDE, or starts summarizing meetings with a new browser tool, they are doing exactly what a productive employee should do: finding faster ways to work. Across most organizations today, employees are running three to five AI tools on any given day. Most were never reviewed by IT. A significant portion connects

items.title: Glassworm botnet disrupted after resilient C2 infrastructure takedown
items.description: The Glassworm botnet targeting developers in software supply-chain attacks has been disrupted after researchers took down its resilient command-and-control infrastructure relying on Solana blockchain transactions and the BitTorrent DHT network. [...]

items.title: Dutch police arrest man over cyber breach at Ajax football club
items.description: The suspect was detained in the central Dutch town of Buren, where law enforcement officers also searched his home and seized multiple digital storage devices, according to a statement released Tuesday by the Dutch National Police.

items.title: Iranian intelligence service behind hack of LA transit system, researchers say
items.description: The hacking group claimed to be a standalone hacktivist crew but actually has ties to the Ministry of Intelligence of the Islamic Republic of Iran (MOIS), researchers at Gambit Security said in a report published Tuesday.

items.title: SecurityWeek to Host AI Risk Summit August 11-12 at the Ritz-Carlton, Half Moon Bay
items.description: Now in its third year, the AI Risk Summit is the leading conference that brings together CISOs, security leaders, AI researchers, developers, policymakers, and enterprise risk professionals. The post SecurityWeek to Host AI Risk Summit August 11-12 at the Ritz-Carlton, Half Moon Bay appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Cybersecurity Evolution: How We Went From Perimeter Defense to AI-Native Security
items.description: The cybersecurity industry of 2006 barely resembled today's billion-dollar behemoth. As part of Dark Reading's 20th anniversary celebration, we trace the industry's evolution through a technology lens.

items.title: RevEng.AI Raises $15 Million to Hunt for Flaws and Backdoors in Software Binaries
items.description: Using an AI model called BinNet, RevEng hunts vulnerabilities and backdoors in released software binaries. The post RevEng.AI Raises $15 Million to Hunt for Flaws and Backdoors in Software Binaries appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: FBI warns of in-person data theft attacks from extortion gang
items.description: The FBI warned on Tuesday that the Silent Ransom Group (SRG) extortion gang is now targeting U.S.-based law firms in in-person data theft attacks. [...]

items.title: GlassWorm Malware Takedown Disrupts Developer Supply Chain Attack Infrastructure
items.description: CrowdStrike, in partnership with Google and the Shadowserver Foundation, has announced the simultaneous disruption of all command-and-control (C2) channels associated with GlassWorm, a persistent software chain campaign targeting software developers through malicious packages and extensions. "Since at least early 2025, GlassWorm operators have systematically targeted software developers, a

items.title: 3 SOC Steps that Shut Down Incident Risks Early
items.description: Most organizations still picture cyber defense as a fortress problem: build stronger walls, add more guards, buy another detection engine. But modern incidents rarely crash through the front gate. They drift in disguised as routine activity, hide inside legitimate processes, and quietly accumulate risk long before anyone labels them an "incident." That changes the role of the SOC entirely. The

items.title: Romanian Hacker Sentenced to Prison in US for Selling Access to State Network
items.description: Catalin Dragomir previously pleaded guilty to selling access to an Oregon state government office’s network. The post Romanian Hacker Sentenced to Prison in US for Selling Access to State Network appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Lastwall Raises $11.5 Million for Quantum-Resilient Identity Platform
items.description: The new funding, led by BDC Capital’s StrongNorth Fund, will accelerate Lastwall’s North American expansion. The post Lastwall Raises $11.5 Million for Quantum-Resilient Identity Platform appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: The Credential Crisis: How Stolen Credentials Defeat Modern Security
items.description: As AI accelerates phishing, session hijacking, and credential abuse, security teams are racing to close the gap between attacker speed and defensive response. The post The Credential Crisis: How Stolen Credentials Defeat Modern Security appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ‘SymJack’ Attack Turns AI Coding Agents Into Supply Chain Attack Delivery Systems
items.description: Malicious repositories and disguised symlinks can trick AI coding agents into silently installing attacker-controlled MCP servers capable of stealing secrets, compromising CI pipelines, and deploying malicious code. The post ‘SymJack’ Attack Turns AI Coding Agents Into Supply Chain Attack Delivery Systems appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: GlassWorm Botnet Disrupted
items.description: Security firms took down all four command-and-control (C&C) channels used by the GlassWorm malware. The post GlassWorm Botnet Disrupted appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Gitea Vulnerability Exposes Private Container Images without Authentication
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed a security flaw in Gitea, an open-source, self-hosted platform for version control, that allows unauthenticated remote attackers to pull private container images from Gitea deployments without requiring an account, password, or other credentials. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-27771 (CVSS score: N/A), affects all versions of Gitea prior to 1.26.2

items.title: CISA gives feds 4 days to patch actively exploited cPanel plugin flaw
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has given U.S. federal agencies four days to secure their servers against a critical vulnerability in the LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin, which is actively being exploited in attacks. [...]

items.title: LA Metro Cyberattack Linked to Iranian State-Sponsored Hackers
items.description: The attack was claimed by a hacktivist group, but evidence showed it used infrastructure linked to Iranian government threat actors. The post LA Metro Cyberattack Linked to Iranian State-Sponsored Hackers appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Dutch police arrests suspect linked to Ajax football club hack
items.description: The Dutch National Police arrested a 35-year-old man suspected of hacking the professional football club Ajax Amsterdam (AFC Ajax) earlier this year. [...]

items.title: Windows 11 KB5089573 update released with performance improvements
items.description: Microsoft has released the KB5089573 preview cumulative update for Windows 11 versions 25H2 and 24H2, which comes with 30 changes, including performance and reliability improvements. [...]

items.title: AI Chatbot Recommendations Redirect Users to Cryptojacking Malware Sites
items.description: Microsoft has warned of an active cryptojacking campaign that makes use of artificial intelligence (AI) chatbot interactions as a mechanism for surfacing malicious download sites. "This emerging delivery technique extends social engineering beyond conventional search results and increases the visibility of malicious software recommendations," Microsoft Defender Experts and the Microsoft

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, May 27th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9946, (Wed, May 27th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: KnowledgeDeliver flaw exploited as a zero-day to install web shells
items.description: Hackers exploited a critical zero-day vulnerability in a server running the KnowledgeDeliver learning management system (LMS) to deploy the Godzilla web shell. [...]

items.title: Feeding Frenzy: 'Megalodon' Malware Infects Thousands of GitHub Repos
items.description: In just six hours, the campaign quietly pushed thousands of malicious commits to more than 5,500 GitHub repositories, stealing credentials, developer secrets, and more.

items.title: Charter confirms data breach after ShinyHunters extortion threat
items.description: U.S. telecommunications giant Charter Communications has confirmed it suffered a data breach after the ShinyHunters extortion group threatened to leak stolen data unless a ransom is paid. [...]

items.title: State Cyber Leaders Beg Congress for More Funding, Support
items.description: A recent congressional hearing highlighted how states are reeling from federal cutbacks to important cyber grants and information sharing initiatives amid damaging attacks to critical infrastructure.

items.title: Shai-Hulud Hackers TeamPCP: Lucky or Skilled?
items.description: TeamPCP, the cybercrime group behind later waves of the Shai-Hulud worm, has done significant damage to the open source ecosystem. But it's not necessarily due to skill alone.

items.title: For Enterprises, Security Remains Agentic AI's Biggest Challenge
items.description: Every company needs an agentic AI strategy, but the tools to allow agentic AI frameworks to be safely and securely adopted are just starting to appear.

items.title: Microsoft Issues Out-of-Band SharePoint Patch
items.description: SharePoint access often means access to the keys of the kingdom, something attackers and defenders understand all too well.

items.title: MuddyWater Uses DLL Side-Loading in Espionage Campaign Targeting 9 Countries
items.description: The Iranian hacking group known as MuddyWater has been linked to a new campaign affecting at least nine organizations across nine countries on four continents in the first quarter of 2026. The activity targeted industrial and electronics manufacturing, education and public-sector bodies, financial services, and professional services, per the Threat Hunter Team from Symantec and Carbon Black.

items.title: Lithuania investigates theft of 600,000 state registry records by foreign actor
items.description: The Lithuanian Prosecutor General’s Office said Friday that attackers gained unauthorized access to more than 600,000 records managed by the Centre of Registers, the state agency responsible for handling property and legal entity records.

items.title: How Varonis Atlas integrates Claude Compliance API for AI governance
items.description: AI governance requires visibility into how AI tools interact with enterprise data. Varonis explains how its Atlas platform uses Claude Compliance API data to help monitor usage, investigate risk, and support compliance. [...]

items.title: Microsoft Defender can now automatically isolate hacked endpoints
items.description: Microsoft is testing a new Defender for Endpoint capability that will automatically isolate compromised endpoints to thwart attackers' attempts to move laterally across the network. [...]

items.title: CISA Announces Revised Town Hall Schedule to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
Remembering Tim Wilson, Whose Legacy Lives on at Dark Reading
items.description: The co-founder and former editor-in-chief passed away five years ago in November. As Dark Reading enters its third decade, we pause to celebrate and honor Wilson's instrumental role in building and elevating the media site.

items.title: [THN Webinar] New AI DDoS Attacks Are Smarter. Learn How to Fight Back
items.description: Every single day, hackers are finding new ways to crash websites and steal data. But right now, something has changed. Hackers are no longer working alone. They are now using powerful Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools to make their attacks faster, stronger, and much harder to stop. According to recent updates from The Hacker News, bad actors are using AI to find weak spots in systems and

items.title: Microsoft Patches SharePoint RCE Flaw CVE-2026-45659 Across Server Versions
items.description: Microsoft has rolled out updates to fix a remote code execution vulnerability impacting SharePoint that could be exploited by bad actors in attacks without requiring any specialized conditions to be met. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-45659, carries a CVSS score of 8.8. It has been assigned an important severity. "Deserialization of untrusted data in Microsoft Office SharePoint allows

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.description: Introduction

items.title: Microsoft Access VBA, (Mon, May 25th)
items.description: Microsoft Access files (Microsoft Office&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s Database) can contain VBA code.

items.title: TeamPCP Supply Chain Campaign: Activity Through 2026-05-24, (Mon, May 25th)
items.description: TeamPCP now operates across three package ecosystems in parallel, it reached GitHub&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s own internal codebase, it trojanized an officially Microsoft-published Python SDK, and it appears to have open-sourced its own framework on GitHub.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Wireshark 4.6.6 Released, (Sun, May 24th)
items.description: Wireshark release 4.6.6 fixes 1 vulnerability and 11 bugs.

items.title: An Example of Stack String in High Level Language, (Sat, May 23rd)
items.description: This week, I&#x27m attending the SEC670[1] training (â€œRed Teaming Tools - Developing Windows Implants, Shellcode, Command and Controlâ€). From my point of view, this training fits perfectly with FOR610 or FOR710 (malware analysis) because it addresses malware from the opposite: Instead of performing reverse engineering, you write malicious code! Always interesting to have another point of view.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Akamai Joins Growing Chorus of Vendors Betting Big on Secure Enterprise Browsers
items.description: When Akamai announced its LayerX acquisition, the company joined a growing list of vendors adding secure enterprise browsers to their product portfolios.

items.title: Cross-Platform NPM Stealer, (Fri, May 22nd)
items.description: I found a Node.js stealer that looked pretty well obfuscated. The file was not running out-of-the-box because it was uploaded on VT as â€œextracted-decoded.jsâ€ (and reformated). The SHA256 is 049300aa5dd774d6c984779a0570f59610399c71864b5d5c2605906db46ddeb9[1]. It did not run properly in a sandbox so only a static analysis was performed.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Friday, May 22nd, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9942, (Fri, May 22nd)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Enhances Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to Include New Nomination Form
CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
